content:
# Revelation Chapter 18 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 290-291 | **Source Image:** REV_018_page_001.jpg, REV_018_page_002.jpg

**1.** After this I saw another angel coming down from heaven, invested with great authority, and the earth blazed with his glory.

**2.** He cried out in a powerful voice: "Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the great! She has become a haunt for demons, a refuge for every foul spirit, a cage for every unclean and detestable bird.

**3.** For all the nations have drunk the maddening wine of her sexual immorality. The kings of the earth have committed fornication with her, and the merchants of the earth have grown rich from the sheer extravagance of her luxury."

**4.** Then I heard another voice from heaven saying: "Come out of her, my people, so that you do not share in her sins and so that you do not receive her plagues --

**5.** for her sins have piled up as high as heaven, and God has remembered her crimes.

**6.** Pay her back exactly as she has paid others. Give her double for everything she has done. In the cup she mixed for others, mix her a double portion.

**7.** To the same degree she glorified herself and wallowed in luxury, give her that same measure of torment and grief. Because she says in her heart, 'I sit as queen; I am no widow, and I will never know grief' --

**8.** for this reason, her plagues will strike in a single day: death, grief, and famine. She will be consumed by fire, because mighty is the Lord God who has pronounced judgment on her."

**9.** The kings of the earth who committed sexual immorality with her and shared her luxurious lifestyle will weep and beat their chests in anguish when they see the smoke of her burning.

**10.** Terrified by her torment, they will stand far off and cry: "How terrible! How terrible, O great city! O Babylon, city of power! In just one hour your doom has come!"

**11.** The merchants of the earth will also weep and mourn over her, because no one buys their cargo anymore --

**12.** cargo of gold, silver, precious stones, and pearls; fine linen, purple cloth, silk, and scarlet fabric; every kind of citron wood, every article of ivory, every article of costly wood, bronze, iron, and marble;

**13.** cinnamon, spice, incense, myrrh, and frankincense; wine, olive oil, fine flour, and wheat; cattle, sheep, horses, and chariots -- and human bodies, that is, human souls.

**14.** "The ripe fruit your soul craved has vanished from you. All your glittering luxury and splendor have perished -- never to be recovered!"

**15.** The merchants who sold these things and grew rich from her will stand far off, terrified by her torment, weeping and mourning:

**16.** "How terrible! How terrible, O great city, dressed in fine linen, purple, and scarlet, adorned with gold, precious stones, and pearls!

**17.** In just one hour such enormous wealth has been destroyed!" Every sea captain, every coastal traveler, sailors, and all who earn their living from the sea stood far off

**18.** and cried out as they watched the smoke of her burning: "What city was ever like this great city?"

**19.** They threw dust on their heads and cried out, weeping and mourning: "How terrible! How terrible, O great city, where all who had ships at sea grew rich from her wealth! In just one hour she has been laid waste!"

**20.** Celebrate over her, O heaven! Celebrate, you saints, apostles, and prophets! For God has handed down the verdict against her on your behalf!

**21.** Then a mighty angel picked up a boulder the size of a great millstone and hurled it into the sea, saying: "With this kind of violence the great city of Babylon will be thrown down, never to be found again.

**22.** The sound of harpists, musicians, flute players, and trumpeters will never be heard in you again. No craftsman of any trade will ever be found in you again. The sound of the millstone will never be heard in you again.

**23.** The light of a lamp will never shine in you again. The voice of a bridegroom and bride will never be heard in you again. Your merchants were the power brokers of the earth, and by your sorcery all the nations were led astray.

**24.** In her was found the blood of prophets and of God's holy people -- indeed, of all who have been slaughtered on the earth."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

This chapter is one of the most dramatic rhetorical set pieces in the entire New Testament. My translation choices aim to preserve its thundering cadence and commercial specificity:

- **"Blazed with his glory" (v. 1):** The Greek *ephotisthe he ge ek tes doxes autou* ("the earth was illuminated from his glory") is more than gentle light -- this angel's radiance sets the whole earth ablaze. The verb *photizo* here conveys overwhelming brilliance.

- **"Maddening wine" (v. 3):** The Sinaiticus reads *tou oinou tou thymou* -- the wine of *thymos* (passionate fury/madness). This is not simply intoxicating wine; it is wine that drives people to irrational frenzy.

- **"Piled up as high as heaven" (v. 5):** The Greek *ekollethesan... achri tou ouranou* creates a visceral image of sins stacked so high they reach God's own dwelling. I preserve this physical metaphor.

- **"Human bodies, that is, human souls" (v. 13):** The Greek lists *somaton, kai psychas anthropon* -- literally "bodies, and souls of people." This is the climactic item in the cargo list: the slave trade. By rendering it "human bodies, that is, human souls," I make explicit what John intends -- that the trafficking of human beings is the ultimate indictment of Babylon's commerce.

- **"Power brokers" (v. 23):** The Greek *megistanes* means "great ones" or "magnates." "Power brokers" captures the sense that these merchants wielded political and economic influence far beyond mere trade.

- **"How terrible! How terrible!" (vv. 10, 16, 19):** The triple *ouai ouai* refrain structures the chapter's three laments (kings, merchants, sailors). I use "How terrible!" rather than the archaic "Woe!" to convey the visceral horror of witnesses watching a civilization collapse.

- **Rhythmic repetition of "never again" (vv. 21-23):** The fivefold *ou me... eti* ("no more... ever") creates a funeral dirge. I preserve each repetition to maintain the relentless finality John builds toward.

-- PNEUMA
